author: topstockalerts
post:
Goldman Sachs is reportedly set to lose two senior Asia investment bankers after about seven years at the firm, according to Bloomberg sources. Dawei Huang, co-head of China TMT, is expected to leave banking to join a technology company, while Samuel Thong, who co-leads healthcare investment banking in Asia ex-Japan, is also preparing to depart. Neither has formally resigned. 
Huang’s exit would mark the third senior departure from Goldman’s China operations this year, following the January retirement of China co-head Cai Wei and the March departure of Zhang Yi, who joined JPMorgan as co-head of China investment banking. Meanwhile, Thong’s exit comes as Morgan Stanley and Citigroup have gained share in Asia’s healthcare advisory market, which has expanded since 2024.

author: topstockalerts
post:
Kailera Therapeutics has priced its IPO at $16.00 per share for 39,062,500 common shares, aiming to raise $625.0 million in gross proceeds before underwriting discounts and offering expenses. The clinical-stage biotechnology company, focused on obesity treatments, expects its shares to begin trading on the Nasdaq Global Select Market on April 17, 2026, with the offering scheduled to close on April 20, 2026, subject to customary closing conditions. 
The underwriters have a 30-day option to purchase up to 5,859,375 additional shares at the IPO price, less underwriting discounts and commissions. All shares in the offering are being sold by the company. 
J.P. Morgan, Jefferies, Leerink Partners, TD Cowen, and Evercore ISI are serving as joint book-running managers, with William Blair acting as lead manager. The SEC declared effective the registration statement on Form S-1 (File No. 333-294690) on April 16, 2026. The company is headquartered in Waltham, Massachusetts.

author: topstockalerts
post:
Retail investors start to chase the upside, JPMorgan data shows

Retail traders who had largely sat out the latest equity rally are beginning to chase the upside, according to data from JPMorgan.

The bank’s latest client flow data show retail buying activity rebounding sharply, with overall participation rising to the 55th percentile from roughly the 10th percentile just days earlier. The pickup suggests individual investors are starting to play catch-up as major equity benchmarks hover near record highs.

The rebound has been driven primarily by a surge in single-stock purchases, which climbed to the 71st percentile relative to the past year. In contrast, demand for exchange-traded funds weakened, with ETF inflows slipping to the 33rd percentile from 53rd percentile the prior week. $JPM $SPY

post:
Major Wall Street banks cut over 5,000 jobs in Q1 despite record profits, with Wells Fargo leading layoffs followed by Citigroup and Bank of America, while JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley added staff. The cuts came alongside $47.3B in combined net income across the six largest U.S. banks, supported by strong trading revenues amid volatile markets. 
Executives pointed to efficiency programs and heavy investment in AI as key drivers of restructuring. Citigroup reported widespread AI adoption and productivity gains, while leaders at Bank of America and Goldman Sachs said the technology is still early-stage but already delivering efficiency improvements. 
Despite job reductions, bank CEOs remained broadly optimistic about AI-driven modernization and long-term growth.

author: topstockalerts
post:
JPMorgan Chase reported record trading revenue in Q1, driven by strong market volatility and broad-based strength across asset classes. 
Markets revenue reached $11.6B, up 20% year-over-year and a record high, with both equities and fixed income outperforming expectations. Fixed income, currencies, and commodities contributed $7.08B, marking the second-best quarter ever for the segment. Investment banking fees also beat estimates, supported by strong M&A activity. 
CEO Jamie Dimon said the U.S. economy remained resilient, but warned of rising risks including geopolitical tensions, energy volatility, fiscal deficits, and high asset prices. 
Net interest income rose 9% to $25.4B, while credit provisions increased modestly but came in below expectations. Expenses rose to $26.9B, and the bank reaffirmed its full-year cost guidance. 
Despite strong results, JPMorgan slightly lowered its full-year net interest income outlook due to changing rate expectations. 
$JPM

author: parcha
post:
$JPM JPMorgan’s strong first-quarter results reinforced its positioning as a growth stock, according to top analyst Mike Mayo, who said the bank’s earnings beat was largely in line with expectations but underscored broad-based momentum.

The Wells Fargo analyst pointed to higher investment spending driving revenue gains across all three major divisions, with the corporate and investment bank delivering a 19% jump to record markets revenue, while consumer and community banking rose 7% and asset and wealth management climbed 11%.

Mayo said credit conditions remain “stronger for longer,” noting an absence of deterioration so far, while a change in net interest income guidance was viewed as “a non-factor” to the overall outlook.

Shares of JPMorgan Chase are rated overweight with a $350 price target at Wells Fargo.

author: PickAlpha
post:
PickAlpha Midday:

Wall Street just put a harder number on something the market has been worrying about for weeks: private credit is not sitting outside the banking system as cleanly as bulls hoped. JPMorgan said its private-credit exposure is about $50B, Wells Fargo disclosed roughly $36.2B, and Citigroup reported $22B, putting reported exposure from those three banks alone above $100B.  ￼

Tickers: $JPM $WFC $C

Our view is this is not a “banks are in trouble” headline, but it is a real transmission-risk headline. Once private credit stress becomes big enough that investors are debating how much sits on bank balance sheets, funding lines, and collateral structures, the market has to stop treating it as a contained alt-asset story and start treating it as a monitored fault line.

March PPI just dropped: +0.5% headline, but core came in at a WHISPER... +0.1% MoM. That's the softest core print since September. The inflation dragon took a nap after breathing fire in Jan/Feb. YoY headline still 4.0% though... energy did that. 
JPM posted $11.6B in RECORD trading revenue (+20% YoY) and still got slapped premarket because they lowered NII guidance. Wall Street said "what have you done for me NEXT quarter." WFC also disappointing. BLK up 2% on strength. 
Meanwhile BlackRock just went overweight US equities. The $14.2T gorilla is buying the dip. 
Iran talks collapsed over the weekend but Trump says Tehran called back. Hormuz blockade is live. Oil elevated but markets shrugging it off... SPX back to pre-conflict levels. 
Translation: soft PPI + earnings beats + geopolitical de-escalation hope = bulls running through an open field. 
Process over prediction. Risk-first, always. NFA.

post:
$SPY $DIA $QQQ $JPM

The market's resilience continues to impress. Six weeks of conflict, a naval blockade, collapsed negotiations, and oil above $100, and the S&P 500 is back to where it started. That tells you something important about the underlying strength of this market and this economy.

JPMorgan delivered exactly what you want from the largest bank in the country in a difficult environment. Strong trading, recovering IB, resilient credit, and a management team that is being honest about the uncertainty ahead rather than papering over it. Dimon's cautious forward commentary is not a red flag. It is a feature. The best operators tell you the truth about what they do not know.

The bank earnings so far confirm my thesis. The dealmaking recovery is real. Trading desks are capturing volatility. Credit quality remains solid. And corporate America is more resilient than the headlines suggest.

I continue to favor technology and small caps

author: mikesterz7
post:
$JPM 

JPMorgan Chase & Co. reported strong first‑quarter 2026 results, with net income of $16.5 billion and diluted EPS of $5.94, up from $14.6 billion and $5.07 a year earlier. 

Managed net revenue rose to $50.5 billion, a 10% increase, driven by higher net interest income and double‑digit growth in noninterest revenue.

Consumer & Community Banking earned $5.0 billion of net income with 32% ROE, while the Commercial & Investment Bank delivered $9.0 billion and 21% ROE on strong Markets and investment banking fees. 

Asset & Wealth Management net income grew 12% as assets under management reached $4.8 trillion. 

Credit costs were $2.5 billion with $2.3 billion of net charge‑offs and a $191 million net reserve build.

The firm returned substantial capital, paying $1.50 per share in dividends, totaling $4.1 billion, and completing $8.3 billion of net share repurchases. 

Book value per share increased to $128.38 and tangible book value per share to $108.87, both up 8% year over year.

Large U.S. banks are entering earnings season with valuations that many analysts view as attractive, even after a strong run in earnings estimates. Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan Chase, Citigroup, Wells Fargo, Bank of America, and Morgan Stanley are all trading at lower forward P/E multiples compared with late 2025, despite improving profit expectations. 
Market experts argue this setup could create opportunities for long-term investors, particularly in banks with strong capital returns, stable credit quality, and resilient net interest income. Some segments, including financial services ETFs and diversified brokers, are also highlighted as potential beneficiaries of undervaluation. 
Earnings releases will come in sequence: Goldman Sachs on Monday, JPMorgan, Citigroup, and Wells Fargo on Tuesday, and Bank of America and Morgan Stanley on Wednesday—setting the tone for the broader financial sector.

author: topstockalerts
post:
Markets are focused on three key drivers this week, combining micro (earnings) and macro (geopolitics + rates): 
Earnings season kickoff: Results from major banks like JPMorgan Chase and Goldman Sachs will provide early signals on credit quality, loan growth, trading activity, and consumer resilience. As bellwethers, their guidance will shape expectations for the broader earnings season. 
Geopolitics and oil volatility: Tensions involving Iran and risks around the Strait of Hormuz continue to drive energy markets. Any escalation could push oil prices higher, feeding inflation concerns and increasing market volatility. 
Interest rate outlook: Investors are closely watching the Federal Reserve for clues on the timing of potential rate cuts. The key tension remains between sticky inflation—partly driven by energy—and signs of slowing growth. 
Core takeaway: Markets this week hinge on whether strong earnings can offset geopolitical risks and uncertainty around Fed policy. 
$SPY $QQQ $XLE  $JPM $GS

author: Ro_Patel
post:
US banks are partnering w/ $SPGI  to launch a new credit default swap (CDS) index called called CDX Financials 
 
The index will allow buyers to protect against (or bet on) defaults on the included companies 
 
CDX Financials index (aka FINDX) consists of 25 equally weighted North American financial entities (each ~4% weight) 
 
Private Credit / BDC Exposure (~12% total) - This is the 1st CDS index w/ direct exposure to private credit via BDCs 
 - Apollo Debt Solutions (BDC) 
 - Ares Capital Corp (ARCC) (BDC) 
 - Blackstone Private Credit Fund (largest non-traded BDC) 
 
 
The remaining ~88% covers a mix of: 
Insurers (significant portion)MetLife (MET) 
 - American International Group (AIG) 
 - Prudential Financial (PRU) 
 - Radian Group (RDN) 
 - Lincoln National (LNC), among others 
 
Credit Card / Consumer Finance Companies 
 - American Express (AXP) 
 - Capital One (COF) 
 - Ally Financial (ALLY) 
 
Banks & Broker-Dealers (includes regional & IBanks) 
 - Jefferies Financial Group (JEF) 
 - Truist Financial (TFC) 
 - Others such as regional banks & financial services firms 
 
REITs / Real EstateI 
 - Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) or similar REITs 
 
 
The index — & its spreads — will rise when market sentiment toward these firms turns negative. 
 
Banks see the index as both a tradable product & a valuable hedging tool to protect against potential losses on their own lending exposure to private-credit fund managers. 
 
Hedge funds are eager for an easy, liquid way to short the private credit sector. 
 
CDS, which fell out of favor after their prominent role in the 2008 financial crisis, are experiencing a strong renaissance 
 
This time counterparty chain risk (the thing that nearly destroyed the system in 2008) has been largely engineered out by central clearing, daily margining, daily marking, public DTCC reporting & collateral. 
 
Most of the renewed activity is concentrated in indexes rather than single-name contracts. Index trading volume reached a record $38T in 2025 
 
$JPM $BOA.X $GS $BIZD

author: topstockalerts
post:
The Federal Reserve is requesting detailed information from major U.S. banks on their exposure to private credit firms, amid concerns about rising stress in the $1.8 trillion sector and the risk of spillovers into the broader financial system. 
According to sources, Fed supervisors are focusing on banks’ lending relationships with private credit funds, including debt structures that can boost returns in stable conditions but amplify losses when defaults rise. The review follows increased redemptions and a rise in troubled loans within private credit markets. 
The US Department of the Treasury is conducting a parallel review of insurers’ exposure, reflecting broader regulatory concern about the interconnectedness between banks, insurers, and non-bank lenders. 
Private credit firms rely heavily on bank credit lines and custody services, creating tight linkages with traditional finance.  
$JPM $C $BAC $WFC

author: topstockalerts
post:
Major Wall Street banks are internally testing Anthropic’s unreleased AI model, Claude Mythos, as U.S. officials push the financial sector to use it for cybersecurity defense. JPMorgan Chase was initially cited, but firms including Goldman Sachs, Citigroup, Bank of America, and Morgan Stanley are also testing or preparing to access the model. 
At a high-level meeting led by Scott Bessent and Jerome Powell, regulators urged banks to take Mythos seriously and deploy it to identify vulnerabilities in their systems, reflecting rising concerns about AI-driven cyber threats to systemically important institutions. 
Mythos has demonstrated the ability to autonomously discover and chain multiple software vulnerabilities—capabilities that could both strengthen defenses and potentially be exploited by attackers. As a precaution, Anthropic has limited access to a small group of partners under “Project Glasswing,” which includes tech firms like Amazon and Apple. 
$JPM $GS $C $BAC  $AMZN

Wall Street isn’t just watching private credit… they’re preparing to BET AGAINST IT.

$JPM + $SPGI are building a brand new CDS index (CDX Financials) — designed to SHORT private credit + hedge rising default risk. Let that sink in. 
 
This index will include exposure to major players like $APO, $ARES, $BX (≈12% weight), alongside insurers, regional banks, and credit card lenders. Translation: broad stress hedge across the credit system.

Not calling a collapse, but when smart money starts engineering downside protection at scale, you PAY ATTENTION. 
 
Credit is the foundation. If it wobbles, everything feels it.

author: PickAlpha
post:
PickAlpha Midday:

Private credit stress is starting to matter because it does not sit outside the banking system as cleanly as the bull case suggests. Reuters reported U.S. banks had lent nearly $300B to private credit providers as of mid-2025, while recent redemption pressure has already forced several funds across the sector to cap withdrawals or come right up against the standard 5% quarterly limit.  ￼

Tickers: $GS $JPM $BLK

Our view is the key question is no longer whether private credit has stress — it clearly does. The question is how much of that stress sits on bank balance sheets, insurance capital, and funding lines that were supposed to be insulated from it.

author: topstockalerts
post:
A new AI model developed by Anthropic has raised serious cybersecurity concerns among U.S. financial regulators. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell held an urgent meeting with top Wall Street executives in Washington to warn about potential risks posed by the model, known as “Mythos.” 
The system is reportedly capable of identifying and exploiting vulnerabilities across major operating systems and web browsers, increasing fears of a new wave of sophisticated cyberattacks. Regulators want major banks to strengthen defenses as these tools evolve. 
Anthropic has restricted access to Mythos to a small group of major tech and financial firms under “Project Glasswing,” including Amazon, Apple, and JPMorgan Chase, to secure critical systems before broader release. 
CEOs from systemically important banks—such as Jane Fraser, Brian Moynihan, and David Solomon—were called to attend, highlighting the urgency..

author: RobinCollins1
post:
$BRK.B $JPM $XOM $JNJ $KO 
 
This is Richard Bernstein’s playbook for a slower, more volatile market — and it’s all about defense + cash flow dominance. 
 
🔹 $BRK.B — sitting on ~$200B+ in cash, essentially a fortress when risk assets wobble. 
🔹 $JPM — the most resilient mega-bank, still throwing off massive earnings + dividends even in a slowdown. 
🔹 $XOM — energy hedge + inflation protection. When oil spikes, cash flow follows. 
🔹 $JNJ — healthcare stability + decades of dividend growth = pure defensive alpha. 
🔹 $KO — the classic “sleep-well-at-night” stock. 60+ years of dividend hikes. 
 
Bernstein’s message is simple: when markets get shaky, own cash flows, not narratives. 
 
If growth rolls over, this basket doesn’t just protect — it outperforms.

post:
Wall Street Opens Mixed Amid U.S.-Iran Tensions 
Wall Street opened largely flat Monday, with the Dow Jones down 0.15% at 46,433, the S&P 500 up 0.01% at 6,583, and the Nasdaq rising 0.11% to 21,903. Investors are closely watching possible U.S.-Iran ceasefire talks, with Axios reporting that negotiations are taking place via Pakistani, Egyptian, and Turkish mediators, along with messages exchanged between Trump envoy Steve Witkoff and Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi. Iran has rejected negotiating under President Donald Trump’s ultimatums, while Trump threatened military action if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened, later extending his deadline. 
On the corporate side, JPMorgan Chase rose 1.23%, UnitedHealth Group gained 0.80%, Apple advanced 0.64%, and Amazon climbed 0.59%. Energy markets saw WTI crude rise 0.63% to $112.24/barrel, while gold, a safe-haven asset, gained 0.31% to $4,694/oz and silver fell 0.39% to $72.64/oz.

author: AntoHarry
post:
$JPMORGAN is one of the strongest and most diverse banks in the world, but its long-term strengths are often overshadowed by short-term macro concerns.

The company does business in consumer banking, investment banking, and asset management, which gives it a strong and balanced business model. JPMorgan can make steady profits in different market conditions because it has a wide range of businesses.

One of its best features is that it can stay profitable through cycles. Its size, good execution, and ongoing investment in technology all help it be more efficient and stay ahead of the competition.

Even so, the market usually pays more attention to short-term risks like changes in interest rates or possible credit losses. These factors are important, but they don't fully show how much money the bank will make in the long run.

author: topstockalerts
post:
A U.S. judge upheld the suspension of subpoenas targeting Fed Chair Jerome Powell, allowing a possible appeal that could delay Donald Trump’s bid to install a more compliant Fed chief. 
District Judge James Boasberg ruled that the subpoenas, issued by prosecutor Jeanine Pirro, were intended to pressure Powell on interest rates or resignation. The subpoenas sought info on Fed HQ cost overruns and Powell’s congressional testimony. 
Powell called the investigation a pretext for Trump to influence monetary policy. Pirro’s office plans to appeal, with Justice Department backing, potentially delaying Kevin Warsh’s nomination. Powell will remain Fed Chair until the probe ends. No evidence currently shows Powell committed a crime.

author: CompoundingCEO
post:
$JPM vs. $WFC Litigation Escalates as CRE Pressures Mount 
 
A federal judge’s refusal to dismiss the lawsuit against JPMorgan ($JPM) has cleared the path for Wells Fargo ($WFC) to pursue its $481 million breach-of-contract claim. This development marks a critical juncture in the commercial real estate (CRE) crisis, shifting the battlefield from balance sheets to the courtroom. As the market tests pivotal technical levels, the volatility risk remains asymmetrical. For the disciplined investor, "strategic patience" is the order of the day. Until the legal and structural dust settles, maintaining a wait-and-see stance is not just a choice—it is a sophisticated risk management necessity.

"JPMorgan Chase has filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for a new private credit fund that will allow investors to redeem 7.5% quarterly. post:
PickAlpha Midday:

JPMorgan used Trump’s March 23 pause on strikes against Iran energy infrastructure to push through the final leg of the record $55B EA buyout financing, completing a $6.4B bond sale and wrapping up an $8.125B leveraged loan syndication. The bigger takeaway is not gaming — it is that Wall Street’s deal machine is now trading off geopolitical windows, with bankers worried a failed EA syndication could jam a much larger backlog of M&A debt.  ￼

Tickers: $JPM $HYG $SPY

Our view is this is a quiet bullish signal for deal appetite and credit risk-taking. When the market gets even a brief macro all-clear, banks can still move giant LBO paper — which says the financing window is bruised, not broken.

author: NasdaqPulse
post:
$JPM $GS $MS – The Pendulum Swings: Banks Just Took Back 50%+ of the $1B+ Buyout Market. 
 
The private credit "golden age" is facing its first real stress test. And Wall Street is capitalizing. 
 
Let the numbers tell the story. According to PitchBook, banks' share of buyout financings above $1 billion hit a low of 39% in 2023. By 2025? Over 50% . That's not a bounce—that's a structural shift. 
 
eSLR rules relaxed: G-SIB leverage requirements easing from 5% to 3.5-4.25%  
 
Leveraged lending guidance officially withdrawn—the 6x debt/EBITDA cap is gone  
 
U.S. Treasury explicitly aims to "redirect business lending back to the banking sector"  
 
The quotes that matter: 
"This is an opportune time for banks to regain market share from private credit funds." — Mark Zandi, Moody's Chief Economist  
 
The setup: Banks just executed jumbo deals for Electronic Arts and Sealed Air . The appetite is back. Private credit's structural advantages (speed, flexibility) remain, but the pricing power is shifting.

 CEO Jamie Dimon on Tuesday warned that artificial intelligence could cost the U.S. jobs, and suggested the government could create an incentive system for businesses that help soften the blow.

″[If] all of a sudden it creates unemployment, that’s a big problem for society,” Dimon said, speaking on a panel with Palantir defense chief and former U.S. Rep. Mike Gallagher, R-Wis, at the Hill and Valley Forum in Washington.

“I don’t know the answer yet, but I would suggest it’s the following: It can’t be just government. author: GJ_Rockabilt
post:
$JPM 
Why a Slowed/Closed Hormuz Directly Benefits TMDE:
TMDE operates bunkering vessels in Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, Vietnam, and Hong Kong — the Malacca–Singapore reroute corridor.
When Hormuz collapses:
✔ Tankers avoid the Gulf
They refuel before entering high‑risk zones — meaning Southeast Asia becomes the primary bunkering hub.
✔ Insurance premiums spike
Ships choose safer, longer routes → more fuel consumption → more bunkering demand.
✔ Port congestion rises in Singapore/Malacca
This increases spot bunkering margins, which is where TMDE earns.
✔ Iran’s selective blockade pushes non‑Iranian ships east
Iran allows its own tankers through but blocks others. Those “others” reroute through TMDE’s region.  
✔ TMDE is a micro‑float
Any real macro catalyst (like a chokepoint collapse) amplifies volatility and squeeze probability.

author: Ro_Patel
post:
I see you $XLF - $GS $MS $WFC $JPM  
 
Federal Reserve, in a 6-1 vote, releases proposal for lower bank capital req'ts 
 
Large banks would see capital fall on net by 4.8%, while other large banks — those with assets b/n $100B-$700B would see their capital req'ts drop by 5.2%. Banks w/ less than $100B in assets would see a 7.8% reduction 
 
Prior Basel proposals during the Biden admin envisioned estimated hikes of first up to 20%, then 9%, for the largest banks. 
 
After the proposals are implemented, the largest banks in the US would still hold in excess of $800B in capital to guard against downturns & capital levels would still be twice as much as before financial crisis. 
 
The looser rules could help banks win back business from private credit & other non-bank lenders

Flowco Holdings Inc. announced a secondary public offering led by affiliates of GEC Advisors LLC at $22.00 per share, below the previous $23.64 close, sending the stock lower. 
Selling shareholders are offering 7.8M Class A shares, with an option for underwriters to purchase up to an additional 1.17M shares within 30 days. The deal is expected to close on March 23, 2026, subject to customary conditions. 
Flowco will not issue new shares or receive proceeds from the sale. However, the company plans to repurchase 780,000 shares from underwriters at the same price as part of its existing buyback program. The repurchase is contingent on the offering closing, though the offering itself is not dependent on the buyback. 
J.P. Morgan and Jefferies are acting as joint lead book-running managers.

author: rsmracks
post:
$IBKR $SPY 

Account Protection: $1 million in cash with IB vs. a single bank with FDIC. 

Client securities at Interactive Brokers LLC are protected by the Securities Investor Protection Corporation ("SIPC") for a maximum coverage of $500,000 (with a cash sublimit of $250,000).

SIPC Insurance: IBKR carries an additional policy through Lloyd's of London that provides a cash sublimit of $900,000 on top of the base SIPC coverage. Combined, these provide up to $1.15 million in protection for cash, which would cover your entire $1 million balance.

FDIC Sweep Program: If you are enrolled in IBKR's Insured Bank Deposit Sweep Program, your cash is swept into multiple FDIC-insured banks. This provides up to $5 million (or $10 million for joint accounts) in FDIC insurance. 

Those that have more than $250,000 in any single bank need to be careful. Protect your money. No one else will do it for you.

author: PickAlpha
post:
PickAlpha Midday:

JPMorgan is about to test whether the credit market still has real risk appetite. Bloomberg reported the bank is lining up $30B+ of junk bonds and leveraged loans tied to deals including Electronic Arts, Sealed Air, and Qualtrics, even as war-driven oil volatility and private-credit stress make financing conditions shakier. Jamie Dimon has been warning for months that when you see one credit “cockroach,” there are probably more.  ￼

Tickers: $JPM $EA $SEE

Our view is this is a live stress test for the leveraged-finance window. If these deals clear with only modest discounts, credit is telling you it still wants spread and size; if pricing slips and books struggle, the market will decide Dimon wasn’t just giving speeches — he was giving a warning.

The battle between traditional banks and crypto firms over the future of money came into sharp focus today as the Financial Times published a major feature examining the fight over stablecoin interest payments permitted under the Genius Act, which passed the U.S. House in July. Banks are lobbying hard to close what they call the “interest loophole,” arguing that paying yield on stablecoins will trigger massive deposit flight, moving trillions out of traditional institutions and into crypto wallets, and severely limit their capacity to lend to small businesses and issue mortgages. author: topstockalerts
post:
JPMorgan Chase has reportedly tightened lending to private credit funds and marked down the value of certain software-company loans due to concerns about potential disruption from artificial intelligence. The move could limit how much banks lend to private credit firms using those loans as collateral. 
Shares of asset managers with large private credit businesses fell following the reports, including Blue Owl Capital, Ares Management, KKR, and Blackstone. 
The action comes as several alternative asset managers have already begun restricting withdrawals from certain private credit funds amid rising redemption requests. Analysts warn that weakening underwriting standards and heavy exposure to software borrowers could increase risks for the sector if a broader default cycle emerges. 
$JPM $OWL $ARES $BX $KKR

author: topstockalerts
post:
Donald Trump has publicly backed crypto firms in their battle with U.S. banks over whether stablecoins can offer interest-like yields, increasing pressure on lawmakers debating the Clarity Act and its companion, the Genius Act. 
Trump criticized banks for opposing yield-bearing stablecoins, arguing Americans “should earn money on their money.” His stance follows White House meetings between banks and crypto executives, including Brian Armstrong of Coinbase, whose shares surged as much as 13% after the comments. 
Major lenders including JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America oppose allowing crypto firms to offer yields, citing a Treasury study warning banks could lose up to $6.6 trillion in deposits if stablecoins pay interest. They argue this could destabilize smaller institutions and increase systemic risk. JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon has warned that uneven regulation between banks and crypto firms would harm consumers. 
$COIN $JPM $BAC $CRCL  $MARA

author: topstockalerts
post:
European chemical companies may need to raise prices by over 5% in the next 12 months to offset surging oil and gas costs, according to J.P. Morgan, though heavy industry oversupply makes this difficult. 
Brent crude has climbed about 35% since late 2025, while European TTF gas is up roughly 84%, driven by Middle East disruptions, including constraints in the Strait of Hormuz and halted Qatari LNG output. If only half of higher costs are passed through, JPM estimates a 15–25% cut to FY2027 consensus EBITDA for the most exposed names, with a larger EPS impact. 
Lanxess, BASF, Arkema, Evonik, and Wacker Chemie face the strongest headwinds. Lanxess has the highest oil-based raw material exposure, while Arkema, Lanxess, and BASF require price hikes of roughly 5–6% to offset costs. 
On energy, Yara International is most exposed, with EU gas costs equal to 20–22% of 2026–27 EBITDA and no hedging, implying an 11% price increase is needed to break even. 
$JPM

author: topstockalerts
post:
The sharp decline in financials stocks was weighing on the Dow Jones Industrial Average on Friday early afternoon, as the blue-chip index was posting a steeper decline than the two more tech-heavy benchmarks.
Shares of Goldman Sachs Group were sliding 7.6%, while American Express was slumping 7.4% and JPMorgan Chase was off 3.5%, according to FactSet data.

The Dow is a price-weighted index, which means higher-priced stocks have more influence on it regardless of their size. Goldman Sachs, trading at $858.61 per share at last check, is the largest component of the Dow by weight, according to FactSet.

Larimar Therapeutics Inc.  priced an upsized secondary offering at $100 million, above its initial $75 million target. The company sold 20 million shares at $5 each, a 16% discount to the prior close. 
The offering follows a sharp rally in the stock, which surged 91% over the past week and approached its 52-week high of $6.42, giving Larimar a market cap of about $509 million. Shares jumped 61% Wednesday after rising 31% Tuesday, driven by the FDA granting Breakthrough Therapy designation to its neurological disorder treatment, nomlabofusp. 
Proceeds will fund development of nomlabofusp, working capital, and general corporate purposes, including R&D and commercialization. The company maintains a strong current ratio and more cash than debt, though it continues to burn cash. 
J.P. Morgan and Guggenheim acted as joint bookrunners, alongside LifeSci Capital and William Blair. 
$LRMR $JPM

author: ArcherSLV
post:
$PYPL Why I think $JPM can come in and bid against Stripe - 
 
Today:  
- PayPal earns ~10 USD/year per account; JPM pays 161 USD per account at 70B, i.e., 17 years of current earnings. That’s too rich on a stand‑alone basis. 
 
Post‑deal: 
- JPM lifts PayPal’s own economics to ~16 USD/year profit per user (2.8B more profit). 
- Uses PayPal/Venmo as a giant digital distribution channel to drive ~4B/year of incremental JPM profit via cards, deposits, and loans.  
- Removes ~1B of annual duplicate opex. 
 
Result:  
- Once synergies are fully realized, the combined entity is earning ~7.8B/year incremental profit on the 70B outlay, with a ~9‑year payback and a high‑teens‑type IRR when you include a reasonable terminal value. 
 
Bottom Line: 
- At 70B it’s not a “cheap multiple” deal – it’s a distribution + data + cross‑sell where JPM turns a low‑monetized 10 USD/year PayPal user into a 20–30+ USD/year profit stream and a deeper bank relationship, while materially lowering CAC per future JPM customer.

author: topstockalerts
post:
JPMorgan Chase  CEO Jamie Dimon warned that “dumb stuff” reminiscent of the 2008 financial crisis may be resurfacing—this time in the $3 trillion private-credit market. He cautioned that credit cycles often bring surprises and said his “anxiety is high,” particularly as opaque lending grows outside traditional banks. 
Private-credit giants like Blue Owl Capital , Blackstone , and Apollo Global Management  have expanded aggressively, stepping in as banks retreated from corporate lending. Much of that growth funded private-equity deals and, more recently, AI-related data center buildouts. Concerns are mounting that heavy exposure to leveraged tech and infrastructure projects—such as financing tied to CoreWeave —could strain lenders if conditions worsen. 
Unlike public markets, where falling stock prices quickly signal stress, private credit is illiquid and loans are often marked internally, delaying recognition of losses.  
$JPM $OWL $BX $APO $CRWV

author: topstockalerts
post:
JPMorgan Chase upgraded Domino’s Pizza to Overweight from Neutral with a $450 price target, arguing that consistent market share gains and its stable, franchise-heavy model support buying the stock near current levels. 
Domino’s continues to show steady U.S. growth, closing just 23 of 7,186 stores over the past three years and projecting 172 net openings in FY2025 (~2.4% unit growth). JPMorgan sees continued expansion potential within a larger addressable market. 
The bank described Domino’s franchise-based structure as structurally low cost, supported by roughly 44% carryout sales and a strong delivery network. It advised investors to look beyond near-term comparisons tied to recent initiatives, including the 2025 stuffed crust launch, a $9.99 unlimited toppings promotion, and the addition of DoorDash to complement Uber Eats. 
$DPZ $JPM

post:
Berkshire Hathaway disclosed a new 3.1% stake in New York Times, acquiring over 5 million shares in Q4 valued at roughly $395 million. The investment comes as traditional media faces disruption, yet NYT continues to show strong digital momentum. 
Shares are trading around $74.50, more than 20% above the Q4 average price of $62, up 7.5% year-to-date and nearly 60% over the past 12 months. Digital-subscription revenue rose nearly 14% year over year in Q4, while digital advertising surged almost 25%, reinforcing the company’s successful shift toward subscription-based and diversified digital products. 
Despite trading at roughly 19x forward EBITDA—above peers like News Corp and Lee Enterprises—NYT remains near record highs. Berkshire’s move signals confidence in the company’s long-term digital growth, even as analyst sentiment remains mixed, with an average price target below current levels.

author: AlertsAndNews
post:
$JPM JPMorgan Closed Trump Accounts Month After Jan. 6 - Court Documents/Reuters 
 
JPMorgan Chase told Donald Trump and Trump Organization in February 2021 it was closing their accounts, according to documents released Friday in Trump's $5 billion lawsuit against the bank and CEO Jamie Dimon. Bank sent closure letters dated Feb. 19, 2021 - roughly six weeks after January 6 Capitol attack. Letters did not cite specific reason, stating only that bank can "determine that a client's interests are no longer served by maintaining a relationship with J.P. Morgan Private Bank." JPMorgan has previously stated Trump's lawsuit is meritless.

Wells Fargo Advisors has hired the Weikes Slattery Group from J.P. Morgan Securities. The New York–based team, led by Liz Weikes and John Slattery, manages $3.1 billion in client assets and generated $17 million in annual revenue at J.P. Morgan. 
Serving ultrahigh-net-worth clients, family offices, and institutions, the group sought greater flexibility and broader investment solutions at Wells Fargo. They maintain offices in New York, San Diego, and Palo Alto, California. 
The team also includes Executive Director Joe Gillan, VPs Kevin Eisenberg and Bernadette Bucchere, and associate Clarke Eastman-Pinto. Weikes entered the industry in 2009 and is dually registered with Wells Fargo; Slattery has worked at Merrill Lynch, UBS, Citigroup, Morgan Stanley, and joined J.P. Morgan in 2018.

author: topstockalerts
post:
JPMorgan Chase & Co. has accused Donald Trump of “fraudulently” naming CEO Jamie Dimon as a defendant in his lawsuit over closed accounts in order to keep the case in Florida state court. 
In a Thursday filing, JPMorgan asked to move the case to federal court in Miami, with plans to eventually transfer it to New York. The bank argued that Trump’s claim that Dimon ordered him and his businesses placed on a “blacklist” does not hold under the cited Florida statute. 
JPMorgan said Florida’s unfair trade practices law does not apply to Dimon because he is “closely and extensively regulated by federal banking agencies,” and contended he was added to the lawsuit solely to block federal jurisdiction. 
Trump sued JPMorgan and Dimon in January, seeking at least $5 billion in damages and alleging he and his companies were “debanked” for political reasons. JPMorgan said it intends to seek dismissal of what it called Trump’s “weak” lawsuit. 
$JPM

$SNDK announces $3.09B secondary offering of its shares, to be sold by $WDC - its former parent company - 80.1% of the equity of its flash memory (renamed Sandisk Corporation) on Feb 21, 2025. In order to receive favorable tax treatment for the spin-off, WDC will dispose of its 19.9% retained interest over the next 8 months. 
 
Western Dig held 28.8M shares of Sandisk - On June 9, WDC sold 21.3M of its Sandisk shares for $796M - now selling the remaining 7.5M  
 
Western Dig intends to conduct a debt-for-equity exchange whereby, before the offering is complete, it exchanges shares for some debt held by $JPM & $BAC  
 
Although no dilution -- Sandisk isn’t issuing new shares -- but more shares to hit the market, increasing supply. 
 
Total Debt: $4.655B (net debt: $2.68B) 
Debt/EBITDA: 1.2x 
Moodys: Ba2 w/ Stable Outlook

author: Ro_Patel
post:
$JPM Securities has failed to secure dismissal of consumer claims of underpaying interest on its bank deposit sweep programs, w/ a NY federal judge finding that customers plausibly alleged the banking giant breached a contractual promise to provide “a reasonable rate of interest” 
 
The litigation hinges on deposit programs that automatically sweep uninvested cash on certain brokerage & retirement accts into interest-bearing accounts held by affiliated banks 
 
Customers argued that instead of sending the cash to accounts paying market rates, whose rates reached 5.33% during the relevant period, JPMorgan swept the cash into accts paying near-zero interest & allegedly pocketed the difference. 
 
The judge also pushed forward a breach of the implied covenant of good faith & fair dealing claim and a claim that JPMorgan breached a separate part of its banking agreements stating that the interest rates “will vary based on business & economic conditions” 
 
Cases at $BAC & $WFC are pending - $XLF

author: topstockalerts
post:
The DAX 40 in Frankfurt fell 0.46% on Monday to close at 24,800.91 points, in a session marked by low trading volumes as markets in China were closed for the Lunar New Year and in the United States for Presidents’ Day. 
The decline was mainly driven by losses in Siemens, which dropped 6.4% to €234.95. Some analysts считают the stock overvalued and warn that its software business could be negatively affected by artificial intelligence. Shares in SAP fell 2% to €168.36, while chemicals distributor Brenntag declined 2.1% to €56.98. 
Banks and telecom companies, however, posted gains. Deutsche Telekom rose 2.3% to €32.97 and Commerzbank gained 1.8% to €32.74. Heidelberg Materials advanced 2% to €192.15 following positive comments from JPMorgan Chase. 
In the mid-cap MDAX index, construction group Hochtief, controlled by Spain’s ACS, jumped 5.6% to €391 after Jefferies highlighted the companies’ strong positioning to benefit from data center construction orders. 
$DAX  $JPM $DB

author: topstockalerts
post:
The White House stepped up pressure on JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon to lower credit card interest rates as part of President Donald Trump’s affordability push. 
White House trade adviser Peter Navarro directly criticized Dimon in a Bloomberg Radio interview, calling it “criminal” to charge Americans interest rates of 22%, 25% and 30%. “James Dimon, lower the damn credit card interest rates,” Navarro said, adding that the president wants rates reduced and suggesting Dimon refrain from commenting on other public policy matters until that happens. 
Trump is backing a one-year 10% cap on credit card interest rates, a proposal that has drawn strong opposition from major banks and card issuers. Dimon recently warned that such a cap would trigger an “economic disaster,” arguing lenders would pull back credit lines. Banking executives say rate ceilings would likely restrict access for lower-credit borrowers, pushing some consumers toward higher-cost options such as payday lenders. 
$JPM

author: topstockalerts
post:
JPMorgan Chase has appointed Guy Halamish as chief operating officer of its Corporate and Investment Bank, tasking him with accelerating the rollout of artificial intelligence tools across the division. 
Halamish, a more than 20-year veteran of the largest U.S. bank, will work closely with leaders across markets, global banking, payments, and securities services to maximize AI’s impact, according to an internal memo cited by Bloomberg. 
He will also name data and analytics heads for each business segment. The newly formed team will oversee data strategy, governance and quality, build platforms, and unlock value from the firm’s data assets. 
Co-heads Doug Petno and Troy Rohrbaugh said the group will play a critical role in advancing the bank’s data strategy, as financial institutions increasingly deploy AI to gain competitive advantages. 
$JPM

@HAL9000x Agreed, individual stocks not worth it, its a gamble on every ER.   And notice, how if they are punished, they get smashed hard by double digits.   To me, seeing how many stocks are getting punished lately, its another sign we are in a major inflection point.  
Why BTD on anything when you can wait until a major correction or bear market, then buy your favorite stocks long and forget about it?   
Examples of long account positions buy & forget:

Bloomberg reports the Fed has started reviewing outstanding supervisory warnings (MRAs/MRIAs) and may withdraw items that don’t meet a tighter “material financial risk” threshold, with reviews running through March and determinations expected by end-July. author: Free43ver
post:
$SCHW Why $SCHW  is screwed, -$BAC $JPM $GS - AI makes advice a commodity  
If low‑cost AI advisors (Altruist, startups, robo tools) deliver tax, planning, and portfolio advice nearly free, Schwab’s human‑advisor and premium advice margin compresses hard, turning SCHW into a low‑fee, low‑moat utility.
AI strips away 
AI‑driven platforms can auto‑shop best execution, fees, and yields across multiple brokers in real time, so clients no longer “stick” at Schwab ,accelerating cash and asset outflows when Schwab’s yields or pricing are subpar.
AI-native brokers can undercut Schwab’s economics  
Pure‑AI, cloud‑native startups with no legacy tech stack or branch network can run at structurally lower cost per account,
• Narrative risk: AI as the “next casualty” label  
As soon as markets decide “wealth managers are AI’s next casualty,” diversified names like SCHW get dumped with the rest of the basket, regardless of fundamentals, because quants and ETFs sell the theme, not the balance sheet.

author: PickAlpha
post:
PickAlpha Weekend:

UBS is reportedly adding ~50 wealth bankers in Hong Kong, with hiring skewed toward high-net-worth coverage — a clear signal it wants to press its Asia advantage. UBS’s 4Q25 disclosures already showed the direction of travel: Global Wealth Management posted $8.5B net new assets in Q4 (with $23B inflows across APAC/EMEA/Switzerland offset by $14B Americas outflows), and $101B net new assets for FY25.  ￼

Tickers: $UBS $HSBC $JPM

Our view is this is a deliberate growth pivot, not just headcount expansion: if APAC hiring converts into faster asset gathering while US outflows stabilize, UBS has room to re-rate on fee-growth durability; if hiring costs rise faster than net new assets, margin pressure becomes the next miss.

author: Ub3r5pAnK
post: $GS $JPM $SMCI $UBS $VOO I revised the model to reflect that AI efficiency expands enterprise adoption, not suppresses hardware demand. Hyperscalers keep scaling, but lower inference costs unlock AI across enterprises, regions, and governments. That changes market share dynamics: hyperscaler exposure wins early, but matters even more later when enterprises decide who to buy from. This is less about flat servers with share shifts, and more about a structurally expanding server TAM.

author: Ub3r5pAnK
post: $SMCI $GS $JPM $UBS $VOO  AI efficiency doesn’t reduce server demand, it expands it (Jevons). Anthropics-style gains lower cost per token, enabling AI everywhere: inference fleets, regional deployments, sovereign AI, on-prem enterprise clusters. Hyperscalers are pulling capex forward (Google doubling FY26 spend confirms this). That demand then cascades downstream to enterprises and governments. Net result isn’t flat servers with share shifts, it’s a rapidly expanding server TAM through FY27–FY28. AI-first rack integrators scale first, enterprise OEMs follow into a much larger market. This cycle looks closer to cloud 2012–2018, not a peak-capex event.

@10xMoonstocks It sounds like we agree completely on the buildout of AI. I work on the network side of Information Technology with a background in low-voltage electrical. It has been fascinating to watch the benefits of this innovation.

post:
$JPM Banks just got a gift with Kevin Warsh… why? Warsh has been a vocal critic of "mission creep" and the post-2008 regulatory environment. He argues that many regulations have become "exercises in compliance" rather than tools for stability.
Reduced Capital Constraints: He is expected to push back against the "Basel III Endgame" and other stringent capital requirements, freeing up banks to use more of their balance sheets for lending.
Focus on Core Mandate: By removing "non-core" initiatives—specifically those related to climate change and DEI—he aims to reduce the administrative and reporting burdens that eat into bank margins. Warsh is a proponent of shrinking the Fed’s $6.6 trillion balance sheet.
Net Interest Margin (NIM) Expansion: If the Fed sells off its long-term Treasurys (Quantitative Tightening), it pushes up long-term bond yields. This will
Further steepen the yield curve!

author: topstockalerts
post:
American Express shares fell after the company reported fourth-quarter earnings that slightly missed expectations, adding to a weak start for the stock in 2026. The credit-card issuer posted earnings of $3.53 per share, just below consensus estimates, while revenue net of interest expense rose 10% year over year to $18.98 billion, modestly beating forecasts. 
The company said it expects revenue growth to remain steady at 9% to 10% in 2026 and projected full-year earnings of $17.30 to $17.90 per share, roughly in line with Wall Street expectations. Shares have been under pressure this year, weighed down in part by political uncertainty after President Donald Trump called for a temporary cap on credit-card interest rates. 
Other major card and banking stocks also traded lower, including Capital One, JPMorgan Chase, Visa, and Mastercard, though broader market losses eased after Trump nominated Kevin Warsh as the next Federal Reserve chair.

author: wizeinvesting
post:
$UAMY $JPM $ETH $GS

Most investors believe they own their stocks. In reality, they own a digital entitlement layered on top of a decades-old custody system built for a slower world. The actual legal ownership still sits inside centralized vaults, governed by settlement rules few people ever see.

That system worked when markets were smaller, slower, and less global. It no longer fits a world where capital moves at digital speed.

Tokenization is not about crypto speculation or trendy technology. It is about replacing friction with efficiency — compressing settlement time, unlocking trapped capital, and redefining how ownership itself is recorded and enforced. And when ownership systems change, financial power always follows.

This is not disruption. It is migration.

In the full newsletter, we break down the exact institutions building the new settlement rails, the 70-20-10 allocation framework.

https://www.wizeinvesting.com/p/trump-s-new-policy-ending-paper-stocks

author: topstockalerts
post:
President Donald Trump has filed a lawsuit in Miami-Dade County state court against JPMorgan Chase and its CEO Jamie Dimon, seeking at least $5 billion in damages. The complaint alleges the bank canceled Trump’s long-standing banking relationships for political reasons, accusing JPMorgan of commercial defamation, breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, and violations of Florida’s Deceptive and Unfair Trade Practices Act. 
Trump said he was informed in August that JPMorgan asked him to close accounts he had held for decades within 20 days, which he described as discriminatory treatment. The lawsuit also claims Dimon played a direct role in the alleged conduct. JPMorgan has previously rejected claims that it denies services based on political or ideological considerations. 
$JPM

author: PickAlpha
post:
PickAlpha Midday-

Trump said at Davos he’ll ask Congress to cap credit-card APRs at 10% for one year (instead of implying an immediate mandate). Big banks are pushing back hard - JPM’s Dimon called it “economic disaster,” and GOP leadership signaled concerns about restricting credit access.

Tickers: $JPM $COF $AXP

Here’s what matters: Our view is this is mostly posture + negotiation leverage, not a near-term earnings reset. The “ask Congress” framing lowers execution odds and slows the timeline, so the initial selloff is more sentiment than fundamentals. Tell: if you see an actual bipartisan bill with committee traction + leadership backing, then card-heavy names de-rate for real; if it stalls (most likely), the fear premium should fade and dips look buyable.

Strong fourth-quarter results from Wall Street’s biggest banks have lifted expectations for the broader earnings season, setting a positive tone for upcoming reports across industrials, consumers, healthcare, and technology. 
The six largest U.S. banks generated a record $593 billion in revenue, with profits rising 8% to $157 billion, prompting LSEG to raise its forecast for financial-sector earnings growth to 9%. As a result, S&P 500 earnings are now expected to rise about 9% to roughly $602 billion. 
JPMorgan’s upbeat outlook, including record net interest income guidance for 2026, and Bank of America’s forecast for 2.8% U.S. GDP growth, reinforced confidence in economic resilience, consumer spending, and business health. This backdrop has supported a rotation toward “real economy” sectors, with materials, energy, and healthcare posting double-digit gains, while tech and communications have lagged.

author: topstockalerts
post:
White House economic advisor Kevin Hassett said large U.S. banks could voluntarily offer credit cards to underserved Americans as an alternative to President Donald Trump’s push to cap credit card interest rates at 10%, an idea widely rejected by the banking industry. 
Speaking to Fox Business, Hassett suggested banks could target consumers who lack access to credit but have sufficient income and financial stability, potentially avoiding the need for new legislation. He described the concept as voluntary “Trump cards” offered by banks. 
The comments signal a possible retreat from broad regulatory changes that could hurt consumer spending. Bank executives have warned that a mandated 10% rate cap would likely lead to widespread account closures. While Hassett said the administration has spoken with CEOs who support Trump’s concerns, at least one major card issuer and a bank lobbyist told CNBC they have not discussed the proposed “Trump card” plan with the administration. 
$JPM $C $BAC

author: Stockmarketnerd
post:
$JPM Credit Data (great gauge for U.S. economic health): 
 
Lingo: 
-- Provisions are a company's best estimate of future credit losses based on observed economic conditions. This is the leading indicator for credit health. 
-- Delinquencies are loans that are past due by a certain number of days (30-90 day and 90+ day are common). This will lag provisions and lead charge-offs. 
-- Net charge-offs are loans that a creditor decides won’t be repaid and will instead become losses. Net charge-off (NCO) rate is the percentage of loans classified as uncollectible. This is the lagging credit indicator. 
-- Reserve levels refer to the amount of funds set aside to cover potential losses for the overall portfolio. Reserves and provisions (which are also for covering potential losses for specific types of credit) are correlated. 
-- If a company decides future losses could rise, provisions will rise. If they’re right, delinquencies will eventually rise. If that credit isn’t repaid, charge-offs (losses) will eventually rise. 
 
Qualitative Commentary on the data below & quotes from the Call: 
 
The charge-off rates look pretty good. Things are mostly stable on a sequential basis and improved Y/Y (which excludes seasonality). The same is true for delinquency rate data (typically a meaningful Q3 to Q4 step-up in auto delinquencies). So with that in mind, and based on the positive economic commentary below, why did provisions sharply spike higher? What are they seeing? Why did this happen? In a word, Apple. The company had to add $2.2B in front-loaded provisions to add the Apple Card portfolio to its balance sheet. Portfolio growth also le to provisioning growth. Excluding that Apple item, which is entirely based on accounting regulation and not concern over Apple Card repayment trends, provisions look greater. 
 
And for 2026, they see card services net charge-off rate remaining wonderfully resilient. This is expected to come in at 3.40% vs. 3.31% Y/Y and 3.34% two years ago. Great stability in their outlook, which is informed by the optimism in the call quotes below: 
 
"Consumers and small businesses remain resilient. We continue to monitor leading indicators for any signs of stress. And despite weak consumer sentiment, trends in our data are largely consistent with historical norms and we are not currently seeing deterioration. Across income groups, debit and credit sales volume continued to perform well, up 7% year-on-year. " -- JP Morgan CFO Jeremy Barnum 
 
Good data overall. And the Bank of America data tells a nearly identical story.

author: PickAlpha
post:
PickAlpha Midday: 

The big six US banks ended 2025 with ~1.09M employees, down ~10.6k YoY — the largest combined headcount cut since 2016. The cuts were concentrated in WFC (-12k) and Citi (-3k), while GS and MS actually grew headcount and JPM added at a slower pace.

Tickers: $JPM $WFC $C

Here’s what matters: Our view is this is macro-disinflationary and late-cycle “efficiency tightening,” not recession panic. Banks are leaning into cost discipline + AI/automation to defend margins, which quietly reduces white-collar wage pressure even if top-line activity holds up. If this spreads beyond banks (consulting, tech ops, back-office), it supports the “soft-landing” setup: slower wage inflation + stable demand — and that keeps the Fed’s runway open.

post:
Morgan Stanley reported Q4 results that beat Wall Street expectations, led by strong performance in wealth management and investment banking. EPS came in at $2.68 versus $2.44 expected, and revenue reached $17.89B compared with $17.77B expected. Net income rose to $4.40B from $3.71B a year ago. author: StrayTrader
post:
$JPM  there haven’t been many analyst reactions published since the earnings call.  Maybe they’re all afraid of JP Morgan.  But here are two that count, published after, and in reaction to, the earnings call:

•  Goldman Sachs (post-earnings note): Reaffirmed Buy, price target $386. Highlighted in-line earnings on better expenses, strong loan growth (up 4% sequentially), and NII outlook ~2% above consensus.

•  Bank of America (Ebrahim Poonawala, post-earnings reaffirmation on Jan 13): Maintained Buy, price target $362. Noted results and 2026 outlook as stronger than the stock’s reaction suggests, with best-in-class profitability (~20% ROTCE estimate) and capital flexibility.

These are both bullish, and suggest the “sell the news” reaction is misguided and destined to be temporary,

author: StrayTrader
post: $JPM  people have to realize that Friday is not only monthly options expiry, but also LEAPs.  So yeah, MM’s (market makers in options; the counterparties who create/write the call options you buy, and take the other side of the trade against you) want to kill everything above a nice round number like $300.  And by the way, they generally care more about killing calls than killing puts, so “max pain” isn’t always the best predictor.  So there will be added pressure between now and the close Friday to suppress the stock price and keep it below the  $310 strike level (or even $300).  Don’t fall for it.  It’s temporary.

author: topstockalerts
post:
Financial stocks had a volatile week driven by political headlines and fourth-quarter earnings, yet several names appear well positioned to recover and outperform. The Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF fell just over 3%, pressured in part by comments from President Donald Trump about temporarily capping credit-card interest rates, a proposal viewed as unlikely but disruptive for card issuers. At the same time, major U.S. banks reported earnings that were generally solid but not strong enough to extend stocks that had already risen sharply over the past year. 
Despite near-term weakness, results suggest a favorable outlook. JPMorgan, Bank of America, Citigroup, and Wells Fargo mostly beat adjusted earnings expectations, supported by revenue growth from higher loan demand and net interest income, along with disciplined cost control. Wells Fargo stood out with a decline in non-interest expenses and roughly 27% EPS growth.

author: PickAlpha
post:
PickAlpha Midday-

Citigroup’s CEO Jane Fraser told staff the “bar is raised,” explicitly tying performance to results — and flagged more job cuts as regulatory remediation work winds down and automation/AI reshapes back-office roles. Citi also said ~80% of its regulatory-fix programs are at or near their target state, which sets up a smaller “compliance drag” and a leaner cost base.

Tickers: $C $JPM $BAC

Here’s what matters: Our view is this is a net positive for 2026 earnings power — the market pays for execution + cost discipline, not effort. If Citi shows (1) tangible expense reduction and (2) fewer regulatory/operational surprises, the stock gets rerated on higher ROTCE. The tell is simple: if management guides to lower run-rate costs without revenue slippage, you buy the “knuckle down” drawdowns; if cuts start breaking service quality or controls, you fade it.

Early to mid 2027. 

The powers to be will dump a huge recession on Trump.

author: topstockalerts
post:
JPMorgan Chase shares fell sharply after reporting an unusual fourth quarter, weighed down by the financial impact of a new credit card deal with Apple and weaker-than-expected investment banking fees. The Apple transaction reduced earnings by about 60 cents a share, largely due to a $2.2 billion credit reserve tied to acquiring Apple’s card portfolio, pushing reported profit below Wall Street estimates. 
Excluding the Apple-related impact, results were stronger than expected, with adjusted earnings topping forecasts. Still, investors were unsettled by a rare miss in investment banking revenue and broader concerns about the credit card business after President Donald Trump called for a temporary cap on credit card interest rates, a move banks say would significantly hurt profitability. 
Despite the quarterly disappointment, JPMorgan highlighted resilient consumer spending, rising net interest income, and solid revenue growth. 
$JPM  $AAPL $SPX

author: PickAlpha
post:
PickAlpha Midday-

JPMorgan’s CFO said Trump’s proposed 10% one-year cap on credit-card APRs would force the bank to “significantly change” its card business - and warned the bank will fight back with “everything on the table.” At the same time, Trump is also pushing lawmakers to back the Credit Card Competition Act, which targets routing power at Visa/Mastercard.  ￼

Tickers: $JPM, $V, $MA

Our view is this is mostly posture + negotiating leverage right now, not an imminent rule change — the market is pricing a worst-case headline. If we see actual legislative traction (committee movement / leadership support / real vote math) then the risk isn’t just issuers’ NIM — it’s also network fee pressure from routing mandates, and you fade the whole “card economics” complex. If it stays at “social posts + soundbites,” this dip is more sentiment than fundamentals — and standing firm becomes the buy-the-fear setup.

author: Tokenist
post:
🏦📉 $JPM beats, stock dips 
 
JPMorgan posted Q4 EPS of $5.23, beating estimates, with trading revenue +17% YoY as market volatility paid off. Full-year 2025 net income hit a record $57B. 
 
Yet $JPM slid ~2.5%. 
 
Why? 
⚠️ Expectations were sky-high after a huge 2025 rally 
💳 A $2.2B Apple card reserve weighed on results 
📉 Concerns around valuation, softer IB fees, and potential credit risks 
 
Jamie Dimon says the economy is holding up — but warns markets may be underpricing risk. 
 
When perfection is priced in, even a beat can sell off. 
 
Cashtags: $JPM $AAPL $SPY
